title: Systemic lidocaine administration influences NF-kβ and TNF-α expression upon musculoskeletal injury in BALB/c mice
author: Resiana Karnina
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
Background:  

 The immune system can produce various inflammatory mediators for protective or protective functions of the body due to stress and surgical trauma. This excessive inflammatory response will interfere with the body’s immune system, causing systemic inflammatory response syndrome and multi-organ failure if allowed to continue. Lidocaine as an anti-inflammatory is used to treat surgical pain and pain arising from the disease process and to treat ventricular arrhythmias. This study aims to prove the efficacy of systemic lidocaine injection as an anti-inflammatory drug in BALB/c mice with sterile musculoskeletal injuries. 

  Methods:  

 Sixteen adult white BALB/c mice (male, healthy, 10–12 weeks old, 35–40 grams body weight, and no disability) were selected and randomly divided into two groups: the group given lidocaine (2 mg/kg body weight) and a group that was given sterile distilled water. NF-kβ and TNF-α protein levels were detected by ELISA, while expression mRNA NF-kβ was analyzed and determined by quantitative real-time PCR. This study used a prospective experimental laboratory study with a repeated ANOVA test. 

  Results:  

 The NF-kβ (protein and mRNA) and TNF-α (protein) levels in rats experiencing inflammation due to musculoskeletal injury were significantly decreased in the lidocaine group (p <0.001). 

  Conclusions:   

 Administration of systemic lidocaine injection was able to inhibit the expression of mRNA NF-kβ and increased the protein levels of NF-kβ and protein levels of TNF-α in mice with musculoskeletal injuries.
